data.title: Google to invest $40 billion in new data centers in Texas, Bloomberg News reports
data.description:
(Reuters) -Alphabet's Google will invest $40 billion in three new data centers in Texas through 2027, Bloomberg News reported on Friday.

(Reporting by Juby Babu in Mexico City; Editing by Shilpi Majumdar)

data.title: US Equity Indexes Mixed Amid Technology Rebound
CIBC Sees USD/CAD Gradually Lowering Into 2026; RBC Flags Range-Bound Trade
Fed's Logan Says Modestly Restrictive Policy Still Appropriate
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas President Lorie Logan says she is not seeing convincing evidence that the US economy is moving toward the Fed's 2% inflation target and modestly restrictive policy is still appropriate. Speaking at the 2025 Energy Conference in Denver, Logan also says the labor market is cooling, which the Fed will continue to monitor.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: Corn Futures Sink on Higher-Than-Expected Supply Outlook -- Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais


-- Corn for December delivery fell 2.6% to $4.30 1/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Friday, with traders reversing course on a recent rally in corn after the USDA only cut U.S. corn yields by 0.7 bushels an acre, counter to the argument that crop disease deeply impacted those yields.

-- Soybeans for January delivery fell 2.1% to $11.22 1/2 a bushel.

-- Wheat for December delivery fell 1.7% to $5.26 3/4 a bushel.


HIGHLIGHTS


Stubborn Supply: Grain futures fell after the USDA published its latest WASDE report, with supply figures forecast by the agency reduced less than anticipated by analysts. Grain supplies look plentiful in general, especially for U.S. corn.

"Surprisingly high corn production which drove U.S. carryout above expectations," said Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives in a note. He added that the USDA's world projections also pegged global corn stocks-to-use levels as "historically low."


Not Encouraged: A separate list of flash sales released by the USDA on Friday, covering sales during the government shutdown, also pressured grain futures.

China was reported as buying soybeans during the shutdown, but only 332,000 metric tons. That is a fraction of the 12 million tons that China promised to buy before the end of the year.

There were numerous sales to "unknown destinations" listed, and those sales could be to China, but they also might not be. "

U.S. production numbers [are] still impressive, balance tables well-stocked, and trade doesn't see any massive flash sales to China to boot," said Matt Zeller of StoneX in a note.


Heightened Fight: Prior to the WASDE's release, CBOT wheat climbed in reaction to a Ukrainian drone strike that hit the Russian port of Novorossiysk, a big hub for Russia's wheat exports. The strike gave wheat fuel to catch up with the gains in corn and soybeans Friday morning, ahead of the WASDE.

Those gains disappeared after the report was released, with higher-than-expected supply figures putting an end to any rallying.


INSIGHT


Future Fixes: The smaller-than-expected adjustment to 2025-26 U.S. corn production and yields won't be the last word the USDA gives on the size of the record crop.

"I think the trade will believe a bigger yield cut could take place in January," said Brian Hoops of Midwest Market Solutions. The USDA may not have accounted for the strength of U.S. corn's export demand, he said, or the consumption of corn by the ethanol industry.

Last week, the EIA reported record-high average daily ethanol production in the U.S., at a pace of 1.123 million barrels a day.


In Other News: The U.S. dollar recovered from early lows Friday, even as equity indexes keep falling.

Wall Street continues to grapple with uncertainty, especially when it comes to missing official data.

The next Fed move is getting less clear, with increasing bets on a pause in interest rate cutting.

"We remain comfortable with our view that the Fed will not cut rates again under Chair Powell," Bank of America's Adarsh Sinha and Claudio Piron said in a note. They forecast cuts in June, September and December 2026, for a 3%-3.25% terminal rate.

For grains, a lower dollar makes U.S. grains more competitive on the world export market.


AHEAD


-- The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly grain export inspections report at 11 a.m. EST Monday.

-- The EIA is due to release its weekly ethanol production and stocks report at 10:30 a.m. EST Wednesday.

-- The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly export sales report at 8:30 a.m. EST Thursday.


-- Paulo Trevisani contributed to this article.


Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-14-25 1556ET
